steps.question: Are training records current for all employees?
steps.description: Verify new miner, experienced miner, and annual refresher records are complete and current for every active employee including temporary and seasonal workers.
steps.action: Pull training records for all active employees. Check new miner (24 hrs/90 days), experienced miner (8 hrs before work), and annual refresher (8 hrs within 12 months).
check: Every employee has applicable training type documented
Annual refresher is within 12-month window
All required fields present: trainee name, content, trainer, dates, duration, competency evaluation
No employees on extended leave or light duty with expired refreshers

steps.question: Is the training plan approved and up to date?
steps.description: Review the written training plan required under 30 CFR 46.3 for completeness and currency.
steps.action: Locate the training plan. Verify it includes mine name, MSHA ID, responsible person, competent persons list, training methods, and effectiveness evaluation procedure.
check: Plan is available at the mine site for inspection
Competent persons listed are current employees
Plan reflects current operations, equipment, and hazards
Plan has been reviewed and signed with current date

steps.question: Are task training records in place for all assigned tasks?
steps.description: Verify each employee has documented task training for every task they perform, per 30 CFR 46.7.
steps.action: Create or review task training matrix mapping job roles to required task training. Check each employee's records against their current duties.
check: Task training matrix exists and covers all equipment and procedures
Each employee has documented task training for assigned tasks
Records are specific (equipment ID, location, procedures covered)
Competency evaluation documented for each task

steps.question: Are contractors and visitors receiving site-specific hazard awareness training?
steps.description: Under 30 CFR 46.11, all persons entering the mine site must receive hazard awareness training before going to the site.
steps.action: Review contractor and visitor sign-in records. Verify hazard awareness content is documented and delivered before site access.
check: Sign-in records exist for all contractors and visitors
Hazard awareness content documented (site hazards, emergency procedures, PPE, restricted areas)
Long-term contractors have experienced miner level training
Delivery drivers receive at minimum a hazard awareness briefing

steps.question: Is annual refresher content relevant and updated?
steps.description: Verify refresher training content is current, site-specific, and includes a mix of instructional methods.
steps.action: Review refresher training materials and delivery records. Check for updates reflecting past year incidents and site changes.
check: Content updated within past 12 months
Includes review of incidents and near misses from past year
Includes hands-on or practical components (not just lecture)
Content is site-specific with local equipment and procedure references

troubleshooting_guide.title: Training Compliance Verification
troubleshooting_guide.description: Step-by-step guide to verify MSHA Part 46 training compliance and identify gaps before they become citations

steps.question: Are training records current for all employees?
steps.details: Pull the training records for every active employee (including temporary and seasonal workers). Verify that each person has a completed record for their applicable training type (new miner or experienced miner) and that their most recent annual refresher is within the 12-month window.
what_to_check: New miner training: 24 hours documented within 90 days of hire, with minimum 4 hours before beginning work
Experienced miner training: 8 hours documented before starting work at this mine
Annual refresher: 8 hours completed within the last 12 months from date of last refresher or initial training
All required fields present: trainee name, content covered, trainer name, dates, duration, competency evaluation
common_findings: Refresher training expired for employees who were on extended leave or light duty
New hires completed orientation but the 24-hour new miner training was never finished within 90 days
Records exist but are missing competency evaluation documentation
corrective_actions: Schedule immediate refresher training for any employee past the 12-month deadline
Complete remaining new miner training hours for anyone still within the 90-day window
For employees past the 90-day window without completed training, stop work until training is completed
Add competency evaluation documentation to any records where it is missing

steps.question: Is the training plan approved and up to date?
steps.details: Review the written training plan required under 30 CFR 46.3. The plan must be available at the mine site and must reflect current operations, trainers, and training methods.
what_to_check: Written plan exists and is available at the mine site
Plan includes mine name, MSHA ID, and responsible person for training
Plan lists all competent persons by name and area of expertise
Plan describes training methods, materials, and approximate duration for each training type
Plan has been reviewed and updated to reflect any changes in operations, equipment, or hazards in the past year
Plan includes procedure for evaluating training effectiveness
common_findings: Training plan exists but lists former employees as competent persons who are no longer on site
Plan does not reflect new equipment or process changes made in the past year
Plan is stored in a manager's office and not readily available during an MSHA inspection
corrective_actions: Update the competent person list to reflect current personnel
Revise training content descriptions to include new equipment, processes, or hazards
Place a copy of the plan in a location accessible to MSHA inspectors and miners (e.g., safety office, control room)
Have the plan reviewed and signed by the responsible person with the current date

steps.question: Are task training records in place for all assigned tasks?
steps.details: Review task training records for each employee against their current job duties. Every task that involves specific equipment operation or exposure to specific hazards requires documented task training under 30 CFR 46.7.
what_to_check: List of tasks requiring training is current and covers all equipment and procedures at the site
Each employee has documented task training for every task they are assigned to perform
Task training records include the specific equipment or procedure trained on, not just generic descriptions
Records show competency evaluation (observation, demonstration, quiz, or practical test)
Task training was documented when employees changed positions, departments, or were assigned new equipment
common_findings: Inadequate task training records is the single most common Part 46 citation; many plants train informally but do not document it
Employees transferred between departments without task training for new equipment
New equipment was installed but task training for operators was not documented
Generic task training records that say 'conveyor operation' without specifying which conveyors or what content was covered
corrective_actions: Create a task training matrix mapping each job role to required task training records
Conduct and document task training for any gaps identified in the matrix
Ensure task training records are specific: include equipment ID, location, and specific procedures covered
Implement a process to trigger task training documentation whenever an employee changes roles or new equipment is installed

steps.question: Are contractors and visitors receiving site-specific hazard awareness training?
steps.details: Under 30 CFR 46.11, all persons who enter the mine site must receive site-specific hazard awareness training before going to the mine site. This includes contractors, delivery drivers, visitors, inspectors, and any other non-employees.
what_to_check: A site-specific hazard awareness program exists and covers: site hazards, emergency procedures, PPE requirements, restricted areas
Sign-in records exist for all contractors and visitors showing hazard awareness training was provided
Contractor companies provide documentation that their employees have received applicable Part 46 training from their employer
Long-term contractors (e.g., refractory crews, construction) have been provided training equivalent to experienced miner or have their own Part 46 records on file
Delivery drivers receive at minimum a hazard awareness briefing covering traffic patterns, PPE requirements, and emergency procedures
common_findings: Delivery drivers are allowed on site without any hazard awareness training
Contractor sign-in sheets exist but do not document the content of the hazard awareness training provided
Long-term contractors working multiple months on site have only visitor-level hazard awareness, not experienced miner training
Weekend or off-shift visitors bypass the training because the safety office is closed
corrective_actions: Implement a gate-level check-in process that requires hazard awareness training verification before site entry
Create a standardized hazard awareness briefing sheet that documents content covered, signed by the visitor/contractor
Require long-term contractors to provide their Part 46 training records or complete experienced miner training at the site
Provide a video or self-paced hazard awareness module for off-hours arrivals with a sign-off sheet

steps.question: Is annual refresher content relevant and updated?
steps.details: Annual refresher training (30 CFR 46.8) must include 8 hours of instruction on subjects related to the mine environment and relevant health and safety topics. The content should not be a repeat of the same generic presentation year after year.
what_to_check: Refresher content has been updated within the past 12 months to reflect current site conditions
Content includes review of any incidents, near misses, or new hazards from the past year
Content addresses any regulatory changes or new MSHA enforcement initiatives
Training methods include a mix of classroom, hands-on, and interactive elements (not 8 hours of lecture)
Content is specific to the mine site, not generic off-the-shelf material that does not reference site equipment or procedures
Feedback from previous refresher sessions has been incorporated to improve quality
common_findings: Same PowerPoint presentation used for 3+ years with no updates for site changes or incidents
Refresher conducted as a single 8-hour day of lecture with no interactive or hands-on components
Content is generic industry material with no site-specific examples, equipment references, or incident reviews
No mechanism to collect or act on trainee feedback about refresher quality
Expired refresher is the second most common Part 46 citation after inadequate task training records
corrective_actions: Form a refresher content review team including operations, maintenance, and safety to update topics annually
Include a review of all recordable incidents and significant near misses from the past year with root cause discussion
Incorporate at least 2 hours of hands-on or practical training (e.g., fire extinguisher use, LOTO practice, confined space equipment familiarization)
Add site-specific case studies and photos to replace or supplement generic materials
Distribute refresher training across the year in shorter segments (e.g., monthly 45-minute sessions) to improve retention and reduce fatigue
Collect written feedback after each refresher session and track improvements year over year
